title: Standard Denial Waiting Period
text: When a client asks what to do six months after a denial because the credit score improved, the standard underwriting policy says applicants normally must wait twelve months before submitting a new mortgage application. The rule applies unless a specific exception memo or updated eligibility pathway is documented.
teaching_note: An older broad policy. It has strong lexical overlap with the question but is not sufficient because it points to exceptions rather than describing the fast-track exception.

title: Six-Month Fast-Track Credit Score Memo
text: The 6-Month Fast-Track memo allows a denied applicant to reopen the file after six months when the borrower credit score has improved by at least 60 points, the debt-to-income ratio is below 43 percent, and updated income documentation is present.
teaching_note: The best answer: it matches the improved-score, six-month, exception-policy scenario.

title: Identity Verification Hold
text: Applications flagged for suspected identity fraud remain on a twelve-month hold even if the credit score changes. The fraud hold can only be lifted by the risk review unit.
teaching_note: Plausible but wrong for the scenario because fraud is not mentioned.

title: Appeals Board Checklist
text: Borrowers may appeal an underwriting decision by submitting updated income, employment history, and credit explanation letters. The appeal board does not automatically waive the waiting period.
teaching_note: Related process information, but less directly relevant than D02.

title: Current First-Time Buyer Rates
text: The current first-time buyer mortgage rate is 6.75 percent APR for qualified borrowers. Rate sheets are updated every business day at 9 AM.
teaching_note: A common distractor in financial-services knowledge bases.

title: Credit Score Notice Requirements
text: When a credit score affects a lending decision, the client must receive a notice explaining the score source, the major factors, and the right to dispute incorrect information.
teaching_note: Relevant to compliance but not the reopen decision.

title: Manual Underwriting Escalation
text: Manual underwriting escalation is used when automated scoring is unavailable or when a policy exception requires senior review. Escalations should cite the governing memo and the specific eligibility criteria.
teaching_note: Useful supporting context after D02 is identified.
